skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Run Python code through the inference.sh executor with data, scraping, image, video, 3D, and document libraries preinstalled.
skill.summary: Execute Python scripts in a hosted inference.sh sandbox with common data and media libraries.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly documentation examples, but two important risks are confirmed: a curl-to-shell installer and arbitrary Python execution through an external CLI. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked, but it should not be published without strong warnings and review.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly To Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start command downloads a script from https://cli.inference.sh and pipes it directly to sh before login. This is a true positive because users cannot inspect the installer before execution, and a compromised endpoint would run arbitrary shell code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command explicitly uses curl piped to sh. The security concern is clear even though the URL appears related to the skill provider.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Python Execution Through External CLI
high_findings.description: The skill is designed to pass user supplied Python code to infsh/python-executor. This is intended functionality, but it can execute automation, scraping, API calls, and file-producing scripts in a hosted environment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples pass Python source code as input to the executor. The behavior is explicit and central to the skill.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable Code Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes requests-based examples for website scraping and GitHub API calls. This is legitimate for the stated use cases, but it creates data exfiltration and abuse risk if prompts supply sensitive data or target unauthorized sites.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly import requests and call external URLs. The risk depends on user input and target selection, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: Generated Files Are Automatically Returned
medium_findings.description: The skill states that files saved under outputs/ are automatically returned. This is useful, but scripts that write secrets or sensitive source data to that directory could expose those files to the caller.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The automatic return behavior is documented, but exposure depends on what the executed Python code writes to outputs/.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives In Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command hits are markdown fences, package names, or CLI examples rather than hidden code in an executable file. The weak cryptographic algorithm hit at the YAML description line and the network reconnaissance hit at the safe execution note are false positives in context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation text, library names, or safety notes. No executable implementation file or obfuscated logic is present in the skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start command downloads a script from https://cli.inference.sh and pipes it directly to sh before login. This is a true positive because users cannot inspect the installer before execution, and a compromised endpoint would run arbitrary shell code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command explicitly uses curl piped to sh. The security concern is clear even though the URL appears related to the skill provider.

dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is designed to pass user supplied Python code to infsh/python-executor. This is intended functionality, but it can execute automation, scraping, API calls, and file-producing scripts in a hosted environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples pass Python source code as input to the executor. The behavior is explicit and central to the skill.

dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly import requests and call external URLs. The risk depends on user input and target selection, so confidence is high but not absolute.

content.user_title: Run Python in a Hosted Sandbox
content.value_statement: Teams need a fast way to run Python tasks without preparing a local environment. This skill sends scripts to inference.sh with common analysis, scraping, media, and document libraries ready to use.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python code through the infsh/python-executor app.
Supports pandas, NumPy, matplotlib, requests, BeautifulSoup, Selenium, Playwright, Pillow, OpenCV, MoviePy, and trimesh.
Can create returned files under outputs/, including charts, CSV files, images, videos, PDFs, and 3D models.
Provides a high-memory variant for larger workloads.
Supports HTTP requests and browser automation from executed Python code.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and user login before execution.
Runs in a CPU-only environment without GPU or dedicated machine learning libraries.
Execution is non-interactive, so scripts must save charts and files instead of opening windows.

use_cases.title: Analyze tabular data
use_cases.description: Process a small dataset, create a chart, and return a CSV or image output.

use_cases.title: Automate website extraction
use_cases.description: Fetch public pages, parse HTML, and summarize selected fields for review.

use_cases.title: Generate media assets
use_cases.description: Create simple images, videos, PDFs, or 3D files from Python scripts.

prompt_templates.title: Run a simple script
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that prints the installed pandas version.

prompt_templates.title: Create a chart file
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to build a small pandas DataFrame, create a bar chart, and save it to outputs/chart.png.

prompt_templates.title: Scrape a public page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to request a public web page, parse the title with BeautifulSoup, and print the result.

prompt_templates.title: Produce multiple output assets
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to generate a chart, a CSV summary, and a PDF report in the outputs directory.

output_examples.input: A request to analyze two sales records and save a chart.
output_examples.output: The response reports that the chart was saved and returns the generated image file.

output_examples.input: A request to fetch a public page title.
output_examples.output: The response prints the parsed title text and any basic status information from the request.

output_examples.input: A request to generate a simple 3D sphere file.
output_examples.output: The response confirms the mesh was created and returns the saved STL file.

best_practices: Review generated Python code before running it, especially when it uses network requests or writes files.
Use explicit timeouts and save all needed artifacts under outputs/.
Avoid sending secrets, private data, or credentials to the hosted executor.
anti_patterns: Do not run unreviewed code from unknown prompts or web pages.
Do not use this skill to access sites or APIs without permission.
Do not write sensitive data into output files that will be returned automatically.

faq.question: Does this skill run Python locally?
faq.answer: No. It runs code through the inference.sh CLI and the infsh/python-executor app.

faq.question: What tools must be installed first?
faq.answer: The inference.sh CLI must be installed, and the user must log in before running examples.

faq.question: Can it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented examples include requests-based web scraping and API calls.

faq.question: Can it return generated files?
faq.answer: Yes. Files saved in outputs/ are automatically returned by the executor.

faq.question: Is GPU acceleration available?
faq.answer: No. The documented environment is CPU-only.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: It documents a curl-to-shell installer and runs arbitrary Python code through an external service.
